cards.question: Define oxidation and reduction in terms of electrons.
cards.answer: Oxidation is loss of electrons; reduction is gain of electrons (OIL RIG).
cards.explanation: OIL RIG: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain (of electrons).

cards.question: Define an oxidising agent in terms of electrons.
cards.answer: An electron acceptor — it gains electrons and is itself reduced.
cards.explanation: The oxidising agent oxidises something else, so it is reduced. A reducing agent is an electron donor (itself oxidised).

cards.question: Write the half-equation for the oxidation of magnesium to Mg²⁺.
cards.explanation: Electrons are lost in oxidation, so they appear on the right-hand side.

cards.question: Write the two half-equations for the reaction: Zn + Cu²⁺ → Zn²⁺ + Cu.
cards.answer: Oxidation: Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻.  Reduction: Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu.
cards.explanation: Zn loses electrons (oxidised); Cu²⁺ gains them (reduced). Electrons cancel when combined.

cards.question: In Zn + Cu²⁺ → Zn²⁺ + Cu, identify the oxidising agent and the reducing agent, with a reason.
cards.answer: Cu²⁺ is the oxidising agent (it gains electrons / is reduced). Zn is the reducing agent (it loses electrons / is oxidised).
cards.explanation: Name the agent AND give the electron reason for the mark.

cards.question: Combine these half-equations into an overall ionic equation: Fe → Fe³⁺ + 3e⁻ and Ag⁺ + e⁻ → Ag.
cards.answer: Multiply the silver half-equation by 3 so electrons balance: 3Ag⁺ + 3e⁻ → 3Ag. Add and cancel electrons: Fe + 3Ag⁺ → Fe³⁺ + 3Ag.
cards.explanation: Electrons lost must equal electrons gained — scale one equation so the e⁻ cancel exactly.
